#### MTC

# [Midwest Transportation Center](https://mtc.intrans.iastate.edu)

![](https://www.intrans.iastate.edu/wp-content/uploads/sites/11/2018/06/mtc@2x.png)

[MTC](/)|[Research Impact](/research-impact/)

## A new bridge structural health monitoring system can provide immediate and easily accessible data

![]()

A structural health monitoring project took place on the Iowa Falls Arch Bridge.

If a highway bridge fails, the results can be catastrophic. Fortunately, bridge failure in the United States is the exception. And to ensure bridge performance as much as possible, advanced diagnostic testing is needed to complement existing inspection methods to identify damage and deterioration that can compromise structural integrity.

In Iowa, such a diagnostic system, one that continuously and autonomously monitors the structural health of bridges in real time, is getting closer to mainstream use. The structural health monitoring (SHM) development project is a joint venture between the Bridge Engineering Center (BEC) at Iowa State University’s Institute for Transportation and the Iowa Department of Transportation.

This work has resulted in a patent-pending system known as Bridge Engineering Condition Assessment System (BECAS).

The system differs from others because it provides immediate, autonomous data analysis and a much improved method for presenting the data so Iowa DOT engineers can readily use it and also access it later should the need arise.

“The monitoring takes place in real time with very, very little user interaction,” says Brent Phares, BEC director, who has been developing the system since 2005. “The system’s software applications run 24 hours a day, analyzing data collected 250 times per second and only alerts an engineer when needed.”

The fully automated SHM system, which uses sensors and software with several specially designed algorithms, was first tested on a U.S. Highway 30 bridge near Ames. Its success led to its yearlong use on the new U.S. 65 Iowa River arch bridge in Iowa Falls. The system has since been installed at locations in Iowa, Illinois, and Wisconsin.

The new BEC monitoring system is a major step forward as a “complete and quantifiable bridge assessment tool,” Phares says. In addition to providing real-time alerts of a bridge overload or catastrophic event, it provides historical, quantifiable tracking of changes in a bridge’s structural condition.

Phares says the monitoring system provides continuous data that are easily accessible and readily and directly implementable by Iowa DOT staff for timely decision making. “The system can be implemented on basically any bridge type to evaluate general performance in addition to targeted, damage‑prone structural areas,” he says.

Implementation continues on several Interstate and high-traffic state highway bridges. Overall, Phares likes what he sees. “I’m really excited about its potential.” A rigorous economic analysis showed that the implementation of BECAS, which results in better maintenance strategies, reduced risk, and increased service life, leads to a notable life-cycle cost savings.

##### MORE RESEARCH IMPACT

# Digital Twin Framework for Real-Time Structural Health Monitoring of Bridges

### *Facilitates intelligent, hands-off bridge maintenance, operation, and management*

This digital twin framework uses artificial intelligence to manage real-life data, offering an intelligent and integrated bridge management system to monitor activity before it happens. A digital twin is a virtual representation of a real bridge, continually updated with real-time and historical data to represent the past and present, and capable of simulating possible futures. Bridges are essential transportation infrastructure and require periodic inspections to ensure safety and reliability. These inspections are typically done manually, but these procedures can be challenging and require working in unsafe conditions. Moreover, the reliability of the collected data depends on human judgment and experience. In the United States alone, 10 percent of the 600,000 bridges are structurally deficient. In the face of aging and deterioration, bridge maintenance and life-cycle management require accurate performance data and efficient evaluation technology. A workflow that integrates weigh stations, automated sensors on bridges, and structural health monitoring via the data-rich environment of a digital twin would circumvent the difficulties of manual inspection and provide predictive knowledge of the state of bridges, possibly preventing serious or catastrophic collapses.

Researchers at the University of Florida have developed a digital twin bridge that incorporates sensing technologies such as structural health monitoring and weigh-in-motion into an intelligent system that can monitor activity on the bridge in real-time. By unlocking data-driven management of bridges, it offers a dynamic model capable of detecting events that may damage the bridge before they happen.

### Application

Enables real-time monitoring and efficient decision-making related to the maintenance, operation, and management of bridges

### Advantages

### Technology

This bridge digital twin virtually encodes the specifications of each element of the physical structure, along with functionality and performance data. Its utility comes from the fact that it is a high-fidelity representation of the physical bridge that, unlike the physical bridge, can interact with other software and integrate additional data. Since the presence of overweighted semi-trailer trucks is one of the biggest risk factors for bridges, integration with traffic data can be especially beneficial, particularly if weigh-in-motion systems are included. Weigh-in-motion systems contain sensors embedded under the surface of the road that can flag overweighted vehicles in real-time. The digital twin developed here is capable of combining with the weigh-in-motion sensors to update weight information in real-time and predict when heavy vehicles should be delayed in crossing the bridge to reduce damage. The demonstrated sensor integration provides the additional benefit of reducing the need for in-person bridge inspection.

A Data-driven Digital Twin Model for Bridge Health Monitoring Using Feature Fusion and Unsupervised Deep Learning
=================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Vahid Mousavi 1, Maria Rashidi 1 2, Ghazimoghadam Shayan 3, Bijan Samali 1

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Proposed a data-driven DT framework for real-time bridge monitoring and management. 
*   •Integrated feature fusion with DL to enhance unsupervised damage detection. 
*   •Developed an MDFA model to detect subtle structural anomalies in bridge data. 
*   •Introduced GAN-based anomaly simulation for cost-effective damage evaluation. 
*   •Validated the approach with real-world bridge case studies and performance tests. 

Abstract
--------

The progressive degradation of bridge infrastructure poses significant risks to transportation safety, necessitating advanced monitoring and management strategies. Digital Twins (DTs) have emerged as a transformative technology for real-time structural monitoring, offering a dynamic and data-driven representation of physical assets. When combined with Deep Learning (DL), DTs enhance predictive capabilities by leveraging vast amounts of sensor data to detect structural anomalies, optimize maintenance strategies, and improve decision-making processes. This synergy enables a more accurate, automated, and cost-effective approach to infrastructure health monitoring, ensuring timely interventions and prolonged service life. This paper presents a novel data-driven DT framework for real-time bridge damage detection, incorporating two key innovations. First, a Multi-Domain Fusion Autoencoder (MDFA) that integrates raw acceleration data with feature fusion from time, frequency, and wavelet domains. Second, a synthetic anomaly simulation approach for evaluation. The framework employs a transformer-based Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) to generate realistic synthetic signals, which are modified to simulate various damage scenarios. Evaluations on Australia’s Werrington Bridge demonstrate the MDFA’s ability to detect subtle anomalies often overlooked by conventional unsupervised DL methods. A comparative analysis confirms its superior detection capabilities. Further validation on Austria’s S101 Bridge using real damage data successfully identifies and localizes all damage scenarios without requiring additional optimization. These results highlight the efficiency of feature fusion techniques in improving anomaly detection accuracy and suggest that synthetic anomaly simulation offers a cost-effective alternative to laboratory testing for evaluating unsupervised damage detection approaches.

Keywords
--------

Bridge Monitoring

Digital Twins

Structural Health Monitoring

Data Fusion

Feature Extraction

Generative Adversarial Network

Unsupervised Deep Learning

Damage Detection

1. Introduction
---------------

Bridge infrastructures, as a critical component of the transportation network, are experiencing significant deterioration due to increasing traffic volume and service load, ageing, and the impacts of aggressive environmental factors associated with climate change. The progressive deterioration of bridge elements results in extensive damage, leading to significantly increased repair or replacement costs. If left unaddressed, it may ultimately cause catastrophic structural failure, resulting in severe economic losses and, tragically, the loss of human lives. Therefore, the proper inspection and maintenance of bridge infrastructure through Structural Health Monitoring (SHM) approaches have become increasingly important for ensuring structural reliability and safety [[[1]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0001), [[2]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0002), [[3]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0003)].

SHM systems play a crucial role in ensuring the safety and reliability of bridge infrastructures by detecting damage and evaluating structural conditions. SHM approaches can be broadly categorized into model-driven and data-driven methods. Model-driven approaches rely on physics-based models and finite element analysis to predict structural behaviour, while data-driven methods leverage sensor data and statistical techniques to identify patterns indicative of deterioration [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0004),[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0005)]. The increasing complexity of structural systems and the vast amount of monitoring data have led to a growing reliance on data-driven methods, particularly those incorporating Machine Learning (ML) techniques.

ML techniques have significantly transformed SHM by enhancing the ability to analyse large-scale monitoring data and have become a cornerstone of modern SHM systems, offering powerful tools for detecting and diagnosing structural damages. Previous studies have developed approaches to detect damage in bridges using both supervised and unsupervised ML techniques [[[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0006), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0007), [[8]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0008)]. Supervised ML studies have demonstrated strong performance in controlled scenarios; however, their practical applicability to real-world cases remains limited [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0009),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0010)]. In contrast, unsupervised learning provides a flexible and adaptive method for extracting valuable insights, as it operates on unlabelled data. Its ability to dynamically adjust to variations in data distributions and detect emerging trends makes it efficient for real-time monitoring applications. Several research studies have explored unsupervised techniques for the real time analysis of structural monitoring data. Despite the extensive progress made in unsupervised damage detection in bridges, the extraction of meaningful results from extensive real-time measurement data is a significant challenge, which affects the timely assessment of structural conditions and the implementation of appropriate remedial measures [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0009)].

Digital Twins (DTs), as a powerful tool for online monitoring and predictive maintenance, have gained significant popularity in bridge infrastructure management. Various approaches, such as big data analysis, ML, and cloud/edge computing can be employed in DT platforms to manage the increasing volume of input data, and facilitate continuous, and ideally real-time, monitoring, which is crucial in identifying defects or problems at an early stage [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0011)]. By analysing the collected data, DTs can identify potential issues within a system, enabling bridge management teams to make informed predictive maintenance decisions. This enhances reliability, increases efficiency, and optimizes the use of available resources [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0012)]. The development of DTs mechanisms within Bridge Management Systems (BMS) in recent years has provided valuable opportunities to address real time monitoring challenge, particularly through advancements in applying ML algorithms within DTs framework [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0013)].

A DT model serves as a dynamic virtual representation of physical infrastructure, providing a real-time reflection of its behaviour, current condition, and performance. This synchronization between the virtual and physical entities is facilitated by advanced systems with high-speed communication capabilities. These intelligent systems, encompassing advanced technologies such as Deep Learning (DL) and data analytics, enable computers to execute tasks that usually demand human intelligence. They achieve this by collecting and analysing large volumes of data from IoT-enabled (Internet of Things) data collection methods and other sensors, to identify patterns in data structure and make predictions with minimal human oversight [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0014)].

Data-driven simulation models in DTs are developed based on the validated data obtained from experiments/measurements on the actual structure and produce a model to analyse the status of the bridge in real time. In data-driven models, data from the physical bridge is collected using emerging sensor technologies and IoT devices. This data is then processed using Big Data analytics and advanced ML techniques, to simulate the exact functionality of the bridge [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0015)]. Consequently, bridge DT is equipped with dynamic ML-based data-driven models that continuously updates using real-time data analysis enabling the damage detection in real time.

While significant advancements have been made in developing of different virtual models within bridge DTs, such as geometric modelling via unmanned aerial vehicles [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0016),[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0017)] and terrestrial laser scanning [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0018),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0019)], as well as Finite Element Method (FEM) models for numerical analysis [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0020),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0021)], the incorporation of robust data-driven DT frameworks for long-term monitoring remains a challenge. Current DT implementations often lack comprehensive data-driven models for processing long-term bridge data [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0011),[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0022),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0023)].

Additionally, the use of real measured data and unsupervised DL methods for real-time damage detection in bridges still remains underexplored [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0024),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0025)]. A key challenge is the lack of historical damage data in existing operational bridges. While laboratory and benchmark structures provide controlled environments for testing, they do not fully represent real-world complexities due to differences in scale and dynamic behaviour, environmental conditions, and operational variability.

Data fusion is a well-known technique in SHM, enabling the integration of heterogeneous data sources to enhance damage detection and structural assessment. By combining multiple data modalities, data fusion improves the reliability and accuracy of monitoring systems. Feature fusion, as a specific type of data fusion, plays a critical role in extracting meaningful insights from complex bridge data using real measured data and unsupervised DL techniques. Given the complexity of bridge dynamics, relying on a single domain limits the ability to capture intricate damage signatures in data-driven models within DTs frameworks [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0026),[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0027)].This limitation hinders the ability to detect structural deterioration promptly, leading to increased maintenance costs and potential safety risks.

This study introduces an advanced data-driven DT model for real-time bridge damage detection, leveraging feature fusion and novel unsupervised DL techniques. The proposed AI-enabled DT employs a Multi-Domain Fusion Auto encoder (MDFA) to integrate complementary features extracted from time, frequency, and wavelet domains to provide unique insights into structural responses. This framework enhances the DT applications for robust, real-time analysis and damage detection, owing to its capabilities to integrate extensive amount of sensor data which provide a comprehensive understanding of the structure’s behaviour, performance, and status [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0011),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0028)]. Moreover, the real time data processing provides the opportunity for early detection of structural issues, enabling timely interventions to prevent failures [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0029)] and implement proactive maintenance strategies to extend the structure's life, reduce downtime, and lower maintenance costs [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0030)].

The primary contributions and novelties of this study include:
*   1.Introducing a data-driven DT framework with a real-time data analysis and processing modules to prolong service life of bridges, advancing BMS toward faster and adaptable performance. 
*   2.Integrating feature fusion techniques with DL within DT framework, which leverages complementary strengths to enhance real-time damage detection through adaptive unsupervised learning on real-world bridge data. 
*   3.Developing a novel MDFA model that integrates raw acceleration data with features from time, frequency, and wavelet domains, significantly enhancing the detection of subtle structural anomalies often missed by conventional methods. 
*   4.Proposing an innovative synthetic anomaly simulation approach using transformer-based Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to generate realistic synthetic signals for evaluation, providing a cost-effective alternative to laboratory testing for unsupervised damage detection methods. 
*   5.Comparison of the performance with the state-of-the-art unsupervised deep learning methods in the literature. 
*   6.Applying real-world bridge case studies for real-time damage detection, including simulating new data points and alert generation. 

Several experimental tests have been carried out on a real bridge case study. Results show that the proposed framework significantly enhances early damage detection and predictive maintenance capabilities, demonstrating its potential to revolutionize bridge health monitoring and ensure long-term infrastructure safety.

The outline of this paper is as follows: [Section 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0002) provides the related work and outlines the motivation and objectives of the proposed approach. [Section 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0003) presents the proposed solution with a focus on feature extraction and fusion, and the MDFA model. [Section 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0004) describes the complete evaluation strategy including synthetic data generation, anomaly scenarios and applied metrics. [Section 5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0013) reports the results of the proposed framework on the first real bridge case study. [Section 6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0019) compares the proposed approach with an existing well-preforming method. In [Section 7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0029) the results of the second case study were discussed. Finally, [Section 8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#sec0030) concludes the paper with final remarks and suggestions for future work.

2. Related Works and Motivations
--------------------------------

DL models have proven to be efficient in real-time SHM applications, enabling to process streaming data from different sensors. DL models automatically extract features through their hidden layers, and typically demand large volumes of data to perform effectively. Bayane et al. [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0009)] proposed an approach for real-time detection of damage in bridges by leveraging unsupervised anomaly detection algorithms applied to strain and acceleration measurements. Li et al. [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0031)] proposed an unsupervised damage detection method designed for real-time application during vehicle crossings over a bridge. Teng et al. [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0024)] proposed an unsupervised damage detection method that employs an attention-based convolutional auto encoder to detect progressive damage in bridges. Kang et al. [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0032)] proposed a DL-based anomaly detection method has been developed for both historical data mining and real-time alerting. Fernandez et al. [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0033)] also employed a hybrid methodology that incorporates Finite Element simulations to enrich the training phase of a DL with synthetic damage scenarios for damage identification in bridge structures.

The rapid evolution of infrastructure management has led to the adoption DL methods within DT framework for efficient bridge health monitoring. The integration of DL algorithms has further advanced the capabilities of DTs by identifying hidden patterns within the large volumes of data and automating anomaly detection, predictive analytics, and model calibration. This capability allows for the application of AI-enabled DTs in different tasks including railway maintenance activities [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0034),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0035)], building predictive maintenance [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0036)] and smart manufacturing and advanced robotics[[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0037)].

DL methods have also been utilized for synthetic data generation to support anomaly detection in bridge DTs, particularly when real-world datasets are limited or incomplete. Jiménez Rios et al. [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0038)] proposed a synthetic data generation framework to enhance the training of ML models for damage detection in bridge structures. In another research from the same authors, Jiménez Rios et al. [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0039)] examined the uncertainties within synthetic data generation frameworks and the potential application of these methodologies as efficient and cost-effective alternatives to real monitoring, supporting the rapid development DTs. Similarly, Mousavi et al. [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0040)] proposed a data augmentation approach to generate synthetic acceleration data in bridge DTs, aiming to mitigate the challenges of substantial training data requirements in DL models, which are often costly and time-intensive to collect. However, Limited research has explored the integration of DL methods within DTs frameworks for the real time analysis of bridge monitoring data. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#tbl0001) presents the comparison of AI-enabled DT solutions (or related frameworks) for real‑time bridge health monitoring as reported in recent literature.

Table 1. Comparison of AI-enabled DT methods or frameworks for bridge health monitoring.

| Approach | Description | Limitations | Ref |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cloud-Based DT with DL | Uses IoT sensors for strain, vibration, and environmental data. Implements DL for anomaly detection. Integrates real-time cloud analytics for dynamic DT updates. | * 1.Using simplified supervised 1D CNN model * 2.Requires stable cloud connectivity. | [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0041)] |
| ML-based DT model for SHM | Uses fiber-optic sensor and deep neural networks to convert strain output into an interactive DT visualization | * 1.Using simple ML model to generate the relevant baseline data. * 2.Requires accurate and detailed FEM models * 3.Not applicable to all type of bridge structures | [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0042)] |
| DT and support vector machine (SVM) in SHM of bridges | ML and DT model are merged to predict damage or collapse of the bridge | * 1.Using supervised SVM model * 2.Real-time performance constraints | [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0043)] |
| DT and Transfer learning for bridge damage detection | A Large number of damage samples of the numerical models were obtained by using the DT and used to train a CNN as a pre-trained network. Transfer learning was used to test on a real bridge. | * 1.Implementation complexity may be high * 2.Not applicable to all type of bridge damages * 3.Real-time performance constraints | [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1566253525006062#bib0044)] |

The gaps in these methods include real-time performance constraints, technical scalability and optimized DL techniques. This study aims to address these key challenges throu

Bridge Health Monitoring System

Release Date: 02/05/2025

Solicitation: 25.4

Open Date: 07/09/2025

Application Due Date: 07/09/2025

Winners selected from the xTechIgnite prize competition will be the only firms eligible to submit an SBIR proposal under any of the topics listed above. Proposals submitted to the topics listed above by nonwinners of the xTechIgnite competition will not be evaluated. See the full xTechIgnite competition RFI here: https://www.xtech.army.mil/competition/xtechignite/.  
The white paper submission deadline for xTechIgnite is March 12, 2025. White papers must be submitted by following instructions provided at the xTechIgnite link above. NOTE: white papers are NOT submitted to DSIP. Small business concerns that do not submit a concept white paper to the xTechIgnite competition before the March 12, 2025 deadline will be ineligible to compete or submit a full SBIR proposal to DSIP.  
Objective
The current methods for assessing military bridges rely on Preventative Maintenance Check and Services (PMCS) combined with fatigue monitoring crack gauges, which have proven insufficient for accurately determining a bridge’s remaining service life. This inaccuracy forces the Army to maintain a surplus of bridges to mitigate the risk of unexpected failures. Research highlights that vehicle crossings, rather than environmental factors, are the primary determinants of bridge health, with the impact varying significantly by vehicle type.
To address this, the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) system seeks to revolutionize bridge assessment and management through an integrated approach combining advanced sensor technology, onboard data storage, and adaptable communication capabilities. By continuously monitoring and recording vehicle traffic, structural stress, and environmental conditions, the BHM system will enable precise predictions of a bridge’s remaining service life. This innovation promises to reduce reliance on manual inspections, enhance situational awareness for battlefield commanders, and optimize bridge asset utilization, ensuring military readiness and operational efficiency.
Description
The Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will develop a robust, modular, and scalable technology suite for real-time monitoring and assessment of military and commercial bridge health. This integrated system will empower battlefield commanders and sustainers by providing actionable insights into bridge readiness and lifespan. The proposed solution should address a combination of advanced sensors, onboard storage systems, and secure communication technologies to achieve the following objectives:
1. Types of Bridges

The BHM system must prioritize adaptability to a variety of bridge types. These include:
Portable All-Metal Bridges: Commonly used in military operations, these structures require rapid deployment capabilities and sensors that can withstand frequent assembly, disassembly, and storage.
Composite Bridges: Combining concrete and metal, these structures are often used for their durability and lightweight properties.
Reinforced Concrete Bridges: Found in both military and civilian applications, these bridges are vulnerable to environmental factors such as corrosion and require monitoring for cracks and stress.
Suspension and Cable-Stayed Bridges: Complex commercial structures where cable tension and tower integrity are critical monitoring points.
Precast Modular Bridges: Often used for temporary or emergency infrastructure, these bridges benefit from scalable monitoring solutions for varying sizes and configurations.

2. Real-time and Periodic Monitoring
The BHM system will monitor key indicators of structural integrity, including stress, fatigue, and damage, through a modular sensor suite. While real-time communication may be beneficial for specific use cases (e.g., commercial highway bridges), the primary focus is on:

Onboard Storage: Capturing and securely storing critical data directly on the bridge.
Periodic Data Retrieval: Allowing personnel, vehicles, or UAVs to retrieve stored data as needed, minimizing security risks and operational costs associated with trackable continuous transmissions.
Future Adaptability: Ensuring the system design allows for the potential of wireless communication integration in future phases if operational needs evolve.

3. Predictive Analytics and Storage Monitoring
In addition to operational bridges, the BHM system must maintain information on the health of portable bridges during storage. This capability ensures readiness for deployment. Predictive analytics should incorporate:

Usage data, including crossings and load impacts, are recorded before storage.
Modular components enable cost-effective monitoring and allow reusable components to be deployed elsewhere once the portable bridge is stored.

4. Indefinite Data Storage and Management
The system must include a robust mechanism for the indefinite storage of bridge health data. This includes maintaining detailed historical records of usage, environmental exposure, and maintenance activities. The solution should employ secure, scalable storage systems, ensuring data integrity and accessibility for lifecycle management and continuous improvement.
5. Sensor Suite Requirements
The sensor package must be:

Modular: Adaptable to different bridge designs and operational needs.
Scalable: Capable of monitoring bridges of various sizes, materials, and load capacities.
Durable: Resistant to environmental stresses such as extreme temperatures, corrosion, and vibration.
Comprehensive: Equipped with advanced tools, such as strain gauges, ultrasonic sensors, fiber-optic monitoring systems, RFID readers, and AI-powered traffic classification capabilities.
Focused on Storage Data: Ensuring essential data components and other non-removable components remain with the bridge while advanced systems (e.g. communications, power) can be redeployed to other bridges still in use.

6. Energy Efficiency and Environmental Resilience
The solution must function autonomously with portable power sources for mobile bridges and integrate with fixed power systems for stationary bridges. Sensors must operate reliably in diverse and challenging environments, from combat zones to urban areas.
7. Secure, Adaptable Data Communication
Data collected by the system will be securely transmitted when necessary. For initial feasibility, the focus is on:

Localized, onboard data storage with retrieval options for personnel or vehicles.
Secure communication mechanisms for direct connection, selective transmission, including burst transmissions or UAV retrieval.
Flexibility for future enhancements to support continuous or real-time monitoring if required.

IMPORTANT: A prize competition, xTechIgnite, will be used to identify small business concerns that meet the criteria for award for this topic. Winners selected from the xTechIgnite prize competition will be the only firms eligible to submit a SBIR proposal under this topic. All other proposals will not be evaluated. See the full xTechIgnite competition details here: https://www.xtech.army.mil/competition/xtechignite/. 
Phase I
This topic is only accepting Phase I proposals for a cost up to $250,000 for a 6-month period of performance.
Phase I of the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will culminate in a comprehensive report that explores the various aspects of the system. The report will provide an in-depth analysis of the sensor technologies evaluated for potential use in the solution, including the selected sensors and the rationale behind their choice.
The study will also delve into the critical aspects of bridge health communication, power supply, and data management. Specifically, it will address how bridge health information will be transmitted to Commanders and Maintainers in an electronically monitored environment, ensuring seamless integration and situational awareness without undue risk. Additionally, the report will outline the power supply requirements for the BHM system and propose a solution for maintaining bridge health data continuity, regardless of whether the bridge is deployed or stored in a facility.
While the primary focus of the SBIR is on developing a BHM solution for the Heavy Assault Scissor Bridge (HASB), the Phase I investigation will also examine the potential for commonality across Army Bridge systems. The study will identify the essential components that must remain with a bridge and those that could be removed and adapted for use on other bridge types, thereby promoting a level of standardization and interoperability across the Army’s bridge inventory.
The Phase I report will provide a thorough understanding of the BHM system’s requirements, capabilities, and potential for scalability, laying the foundation for the development of a robust and effective bridge health monitoring solution.
Phase II
Phase II of the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will focus on the fabrication and testing of a prototype system, building on the research and analysis conducted in Phase I. A key aspect of this phase will be the implementation of robust cyber security measures to ensure the system’s integrity and protect against potential threats.
In addition to prototype development, the vendor will conduct a comprehensive sustainability assessment of the selected components to guarantee a viable path for long-term support and maintenance. This assessment will ensure that the chosen technical solutions can be supported and upgraded over time, minimizing the risk of obsolescence and ensuring the system’s continued effectiveness.
The prototype development process will involve the creation of off-bridge components and breadboard systems, which will be thoroughly tested and optimized to ensure seamless integration and performance. The final prototype will be designed to be compatible with the Heavy Assault Scissor Bridge (HASB) and will include all necessary equipment to function as proposed.
In collaboration with the PdM Bridging team and the GVSC Bridge Technology Lab, the prototype will undergo rigorous validation and testing on a HASB to ensure its performance and accuracy. The vendor will also conduct a live demonstration of the solution at Fort Leonard Wood, showcasing its capabilities on a local bridge.
The deliverables for Phase II will include two complete systems, which will support additional field evaluations and testing. These systems will provide the Army with a tangible, functional solution for monitoring bridge health, enabling more informed decision-making and improved maintenance practices.
Phase III

Critical Infrastructure: This full stack option can be fitted to provide the same real-time, non-invasive analysis. ​
Predictive Maintenance: Can provide real-time feedback on the performance of a vehicle, equipment part, etc., to save time on repairs. ​
Smart Cities: Allows for various sensors to track various assets at once in one single “pane of glass.”

The deliverables for Phase II will include two complete systems, which will support additional field evaluations and testing. These systems will provide the Army with a tangible, functional solution for monitoring bridge health, enabling more informed decision-making and improved maintenance practices.

Specifically, it will address how bridge health information will be transmitted to Commanders and Maintainers in an electronically monitored environment, ensuring seamless integration and situational awareness without undue risk. Additionally, the report will outline the power supply requirements for the BHM system and propose a solution for maintaining bridge health data continuity, regardless of whether the bridge is deployed or stored in a facility.
While the primary focus of the SBIR is on developing a BHM solution for the Heavy Assault Scissor Bridge (HASB), the Phase I investigation will also examine the potential for commonality across Army Bridge systems. The study will identify the essential components that must remain with a bridge and those that could be removed and adapted for use on other bridge types, thereby promoting a level of standardization and interoperability across the Army’s bridge inventory.
The Phase I report will provide a thorough understanding of the BHM system’s requirements, capabilities, and potential for scalability, laying the foundation for the development of a robust and effective bridge health monitoring solution.
Phase II
Phase II of the Bridge Health Monitoring (BHM) project will focus on the fabrication and testing of a prototype system, building on the research and analysis conducted in Phase I. A key aspect of this phase will be the implementation of robust cyber security measures to ensure the system’s integrity and protect against potential threats.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Real-time data access enhances decision-making in bridge management, enabling immediate responses to structural changes and potential hazards, thus minimizing risks and improving safety protocols.
Predictive maintenance utilizes data analytics to forecast bridge health issues, optimizing maintenance schedules and reducing costs, ultimately extending the lifespan of infrastructure assets.
Advanced technologies, such as IoT and AI, facilitate comprehensive monitoring systems that provide continuous updates, improving the accuracy of health assessments and enabling proactive management.
